coredata.dc:title: Targeting and sensing of some cancer cells using folate bioreceptor functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Soleymani, Jafar
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Somi, Mohammad Hossein
Ozkan, Sibel Ayşil
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, study of folate receptor (FR) expression related to targeting, drug delivery and counting of tumoral cells have been followed. In this work, a fast and simple strategy was reported to determine the FR expressed cancer cells based on the selective bonding of the folic acid/folate (FA) to the FR-positive tumor cells. The folate decorated Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were utilized as selective targeting of the MKN 45 cells. Fluorescent microscopy imaging investigations revealed that the produced FA conjugated N-GQDs could specifically attach to the target FR-positive tumor cells. Due to the fluorescence emission of N-GQDs, the developed cytosensor is free from attaching any fluorescent ligand i.e. Rhodamine B to capture the florescence microscopy images and also flow cytometry analysis. The fabricated cytosensor possesses a dynamic range from 100 to 7.0 × 104 cell·mL−1 with high selectivity. Furthermore, the cytosensor also could visualized the MCF 7 and HT 29 cells where the dynamic ranges were 100 to 1.0 × 104 and 500 to 4.0 × 104 cells·mL−1, respectively. In vitro toxicity tests has shown low toxicity of the synthesized N-GQDs where the minimum viability is 68%. The proposed FA-N-GQDs based cytosensor provides a novel platform for detection of MKN 45, HT 29 and MCF 7 cancer cell lines which could be used in multi-channel cancer diagnosis biodevice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Folate bioreceptor
Cell targeting
Cytosensor
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Cancer diagnosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FA-N-GQDs apoptosis studies by flow cytometry
FA-N-GQDs uptake studies by fluorescence microscopy
Doxorubicin (DOX) treated cells with fluorescence microscopy and the RRS
Characterization of synthesized N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs
Cytosensing of MKN 45 after treatment with doxorubicin

This study was supported by Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Science as PhD thesis of J. Soleymani under Ethical Committee License of the National Institute for Medical Research Development (IR.NIMAD.REC.1396210).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of FA, N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs.
DLS analysis of particle size of N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs.
FE-SEM images of (a) N-GQDs (b) FA-N-GQDs.
AFM images of N-GQDs.
TEM images of (a) FA-N-GQDs (b) N-GQDs.
XRD spectra of FA-N-GQDs and N-GQDs.
UV spectra of (a) bonding of FA to NHS and (b) FA-NHS to N-GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs.
FA-N-GQDs uptake by MKN 45 cells (a) control, (b) 20.0, (c) 10.0, (d) 2.0 μg·mL−1.
MTT assay for the cell proliferation using MKN 45 cell line at 24, 48 and 72 h in various concentration of FA-N-GQDs (0.00, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50, 1.00, 2.00, 10.00 and 20.00 μg·mL−1).
DAPI staining images (a) control, (b) 2.0 (c) 10.0 and (d) 20 μg·mL−1 of FA-N-GQDs.
RRS spectra of MKN 45 affected FA-N-GQDs (A) and calibration curve of MKN 45 (B), MCF 7 (C), HT 29 (D).
Effects of pH (A), FA-N-GQDs concentration (B) and incubation time (C). (Conditions: PBS, 0.1 M, incubation temperature 37 °C).
Fluorescence images of the HEK 293 normal cells in the presence of the FA-N-GQDs with different concentrations control (a), (b and f) 2.0 (c and g) 10.0 and (d and h) 20 μg·mL−1 of FA-N-GQDs.
Detection of MKN 45 cancer cells treated with various concentration of doxorubicin.
Fluorescence microscopic image of MKN 45 cancer cell lines after treating with 10 μg·mL−1 of doxorubicin at 1 and 2 h.
Mechanism of the proposed cytosensor.
Synthesis and functionalization of N-GQDs.
Optical and electrochemical sensors for cancer cell detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SWV; Square-wave voltammetry, EIS; Electrochemical impedance spectra, DPV; Deferential pulse voltammetry. ChA; Chronoamperometric, Fc-PEI; Poly(ethylene imine) ferrocene, SWNTs; single-wall carbon nanotubes, pDATT; polymer [2,2′:5′,2″‑terthiophene]‑3′,4′‑diamine, PGE; Pencil graphite electrode Au-PE; Au-paper electrode, MNPs; magnetic nanoparticles, MUC1; human mucin-1, FA-GSH-GNPs; FA-glutathione conjugated gold nanoparticles, SPR; Surface plasmon resonance.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Lower limit of quantification; LLOQ values.

head.ce:title: Targeting and sensing of some cancer cells using folate bioreceptor functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Ankara University, 06100 Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, study of folate receptor (FR) expression related to targeting, drug delivery and counting of tumoral cells have been followed. In this work, a fast and simple strategy was reported to determine the FR expressed cancer cells based on the selective bonding of the folic acid/folate (FA) to the FR-positive tumor cells. The folate decorated Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were utilized as selective targeting of the MKN 45 cells. Fluorescent microscopy imaging investigations revealed that the produced FA conjugated N-GQDs could specifically attach to the target FR-positive tumor cells. Due to the fluorescence emission of N-GQDs, the developed cytosensor is free from attaching any fluorescent ligand i.e. Rhodamine B to capture the florescence microscopy images and also flow cytometry analysis. The fabricated cytosensor possesses a dynamic range from 100 to 7.0 × 104 cell·mL−1 with high selectivity. Furthermore, the cytosensor also could visualized the MCF 7 and HT 29 cells where the dynamic ranges were 100 to 1.0 × 104 and 500 to 4.0 × 104 cells·mL−1, respectively. In vitro toxicity tests has shown low toxicity of the synthesized N-GQDs where the minimum viability is 68%. The proposed FA-N-GQDs based cytosensor provides a novel platform for detection of MKN 45, HT 29 and MCF 7 cancer cell lines which could be used in multi-channel cancer diagnosis biodevice.

ce:section.ce:para: The folate receptor (FR), with molecular weight of 38 kDa, is overexpressed in different malignancies, including kidney, lung, gastric, ovarian and breast cancers [1,2]. Attention to the applying of FR as specific agent for targeted therapy and imaging have been led to an explosive increase in the introducing of folate based targeted drugs, imaging agents, proteins, and also theranostic devices for the simultaneous diagnosis and treatments of the malignancies [3]. The FR expression is mainly restricted to the cancer cells and the density of the FRs in cancer cells is about 100–300 times higher than normal cells which this abundance arises from their relatively high necessity for FA [4]. FA can attach to the cell membrane FRs with a high affinity (Kd of 0.1–1 nM) to assist the cellular internalization of various drug delivery, imagining and sensing agents. The benefits of FR as the target agent for drug delivery are raised from (1) in normal tissues, FR expression is limited to the inaccessible epithelial cells; (2) FR is over-expressed in numerous cancers; (3) FR could endocytosis the folate-attached agents to the cytoplasm of cells which is the base of the targeted delivery [5–7]. The above mentioned reasons propose using FR as effective receptor target for treatment of various FR-positive cancer cells. Using FR as a targeted imaging tool possesses remarkable properties over the different imaging materials in clinical trials and shows two times better in efficiency [8,9]. FR based cytosensors show high selective manner and accurate results towards the FR-positive cells. Various cytosensors based on optical and electrochemical methods have been reported for detection of cancer cells [10–22]. Some features of the reported methods such as used materials, linear range and limit of detection were collected in Table 1 .

Despite of the effectiveness of reported methods, some problems such as costly and time-consuming experimental procedures have still to be questioned and create a positive motivation for researchers to design novel more powerful biodevices.

Also, the biocompatibility of the materials applied in cytosensors designing is another problem that affects the sensing performance of the cytosensors. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop new cytosensing platform for FR-positive cell monitoring.

Resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) is a sensitive method and recently applied for analyte-detection purposes from inorganic materials to protein detection [23–27]. The RRS is originated from elastic scattering phenomena where incidence of the Rayleigh scattering wavelength to the absorption band of molecules produces RRS spectra. Various factors can affect the intensity and peak shape of the RRS including size and conformational orientation of molecules [28]. Till now, only few works were reported based on RRS effect for biosensing of cancer cells [29].

Carbon based materials were widely used in biomedical applications including drug delivery [30], sensing [31], imaging [32] and therapy purposes [33,34]. In the case of cytosensing, various strategies were applied to detect cancer cells. For example, Dervisevic et al. [16] utilized the pencil graphite electrode (PGE) to functionalize with 3‑Thienyl boronic acid and Thiophen and detect the gastric cancer cells. Also, Yadegari and coworkers [35] applied graphene modified electrode to detect human prostate metastatic cancer cells. However, the specificity towards the targeting of is majer problem cells. The false–positive/negative detection and differentiation between normal and cancer cells could be frequently concluded from the reported papers. However, the receptor based approaches can decease the non-specific detection and subsequently increase the accuracy of the detection method. In the current work the FR was utilized to detect cancer cells.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are fluorescent materials that have been concerned remarkable consideration for their large surface-area-to-volume ratio, broad and size-depended absorption and emissions, good aqueous solubility, high resistance to photo bleaching, exceptional biocompatibility and low toxicity. These outstanding properties make GQDs as promising materials for various applications from engineering to medical fields. Regarding the superior properties of the GQDs and the specificity of the FRs can be resulted in a platform with favor sensitivity, selectivity and biocompatibility [36].

On account of the unique fluorescent properties of GQDs, these nanomaterials can be used for the real time monitoring of cancer cell detection and cellular interaction and internalization as well as biological functions. Interestingly, the bioconjugation of GQDs with FA as a proper targeting moiety could enhance their bioactivity and used for cytosensing.

In this report, we introduce the synthesis of FR-targeted GQDs to selective and specific detection of FR-positive cancer cells. The FA moieties were decorated to the N-GQDs surfaces by a crosslinking reaction between carboxylic acid groups of FA and amine group of N-GQDs. The mechanism of cytosensing is based on the specific attaching of FA to the FR of cancer cell membrane. The schematic representation of the attaching event is described in Scheme 1 . The resultant FA-decorated GQDs showed low photobleachibility and cytotoxicity. The synthesis approach of these materials is very simple and also the produced biocompatible FA-N-GQDs is stable for at least 2 months at 4 °C. The constructed platform acts as RRS cytosensor. Specific detection of the FR-positive cancer cells lines are demonstrated using MKN 45, MCF 7 and HT 29 cancer cells. The cell viabilities of the tested cell lines showed no significant decrease in viabilities which exhibit the biocompatible nature of the FA-N-GQDs.

Citric acid, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and ethylenediamine with purities of higher than 98.0% were purchased from Merck. N‑1‑ethyl‑3‑(3‑dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 98.0%), hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98.0%), 4′,6‑diamidino‑2‑phenylindole (DAPI) and 3‑(4,5‑dimethylthiazol‑2‑yl)‑2,5‑diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldirich Co. Doxorubicin HCl and FA were purchased from Nano Eksir Sina and Mehr Darou (Iran), respectively. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin, trypsin-EDTA (25%) and Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 growth medium (RPMI) were purchased from Gibco BRL Life Technologies (USA). All cells were provided from the standard cell banks of the National cell bank of Iran (NCBI) (Tehran, Iran). Ultra-pure water was used for preparation of the standard solutions and sterilized for buffer preparation for cell culture works. Solutions were prepared from analytical grade standard materials without any purifications steps using double distilled water. All standard solutions were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

TEM (transmission electron microscopy) analysis was recorded on a Philips CM30 electron microscope operated at 200 kV (Adelaide, Australia). The morphology of the FA-N-GQDs was evaluated with a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis which was conducted on FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, Digital Instruments nanosurf mobile S, Grammetstrasse, Switzerland) was applied to study Surface topography of the GQDs and approve the FESEM results. The elemental analysis of the FA-N-GQDs was recorded by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy of MIRA3 ESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). The functional groups of the FA-N-GQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra, Shimadzu model FTIR prestige 21 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) using KBr discs. Crystallinity of the FA-N-GQDs were tested using X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns by a Siemens D 5000 X-Ray diffractometer (Texas, USA) with a Cu Kα anode (λ = 1.54 Å) operating at 40 kV and 30 mA. The diffraction patterns were collected at room temperature from 3.0 to 10 and 10 to 70°. The dwell time was 12 s per increment and a step size of 0.05° per step was applied. Zeta potential values and the particle size distribution of FA-N-GQDs samples were measured by Malvern particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). Laser-scattering technique was used to determine the mean diameter and zeta-potential of FA-N-GQDs by Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments (Malvern, UK) at room temperature. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) of samples was recorded by a model Jade DSC, PerkinElmer (USA). UV–Vis spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu 1800 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Suzhou, Japan). Fluorescence imaging images were recorded on a Model Bh2-RFCA fluorescence Olympus microscope (Tokyo, Japan). A microplate reader of Awareness Technology (FL, USA) was applied to read absorbance of MTT assay samples at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm. Fluorescence of the DOX and FA-N-GQDs treated cells were detected by flow cytometry of FACScalibur flow-cytometer, Becton Dickinson Immunocytometry Systems (California, USA).

Briefly, 0.4 mL ethylenediamine and 1 g citric acid were added into 15 mL water and stirred to dissolve and mix for about 1 min. Afterwards, the mixture was placed into 20 mL Teflon lined stainless hydrothermal synthesis autoclave and then the autoclave sealed and heated to 180 °C in oven and kept for 8 h. Finally, to remove any insoluble materials, the synthesized solution centrifuged 10.000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained N-GQDs were stored at 4 °C for further uses (Scheme 2 ) [37].

Functionalization of carboxyl-ended N-GQDs with FA was accomplished through carbodiimide chemistry and addition of 1‑ethyl‑3‑(3‑dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N‑hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) to N-GQDs with final concentration of 50 mg·mL−1. Briefly, 1 mg of EDC, 1.5 mg of NHS and 2.3 mg of FA were mixed in PBS buffer (10 mL, pH 7.5) at room temperature and stirred for about 15 h. Then, 1 mL of synthesized N-GQDs was added to the reaction mixture and stirred for 24 h (Scheme 2). Finally, the FA-N-GQDs mixture was centrifuged to remove the floating substances and stored at 4 °C.

HEK 293, MKN 45, MCF 7 and HT 29 were cultured in RPMI culture media which supplemented with 10% FBS at 37 °C under 5% CO2. At the end of the growth period, cell lines were washed with PBS to remove the excess RPMI and then detached by trypsin and incubated for 5 min at 37 °C under 5% CO2. Afterwards, the cells were transferred to the centrifuge tube to remove excess trypsin. Finally, the cell lines were centrifuged and resuspended to the new medium for further works.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FA-N-GQDs apoptosis studies by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: The uptake of MKN 45 cancer cell by folate attached N-GQDs were considered by flow cytometry. Firstly, about 5 × 105 cells of MKN 45 were seeded in each well of a 6-well tissue-culture plate. To reach the maximum density of cell/well, the MKN 45 cell lines were incubated for 24 h and then cell lines were affected by FA-N-GQDs and incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 for another 20 h. Afterwards, cells were washed with PBS and treated with trypsin to remove and suspend in the fresh medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FA-N-GQDs uptake studies by fluorescence microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: About 5 × 105 cells/well of MKN 45 cell lines were seeded into a 6-well tissue-culture plate. After 24 h, MKN 45 cells were incubated in the presence or absence of FA-N-GQDs for 20 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2. Then, the culture medium was disposed and MKN 45 was washed with PBS for three times to remove the excess culture medium. The fluorescence microscopy images were taken to demonstrate the uptake of FA-N-GQDs using glass slides to fix the cells lines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Doxorubicin (DOX) treated cells with fluorescence microscopy and the RRS
ce:section.ce:para: The cell culture of MKN 45 cell lines were carried out by seeding of 5 × 105 cells to the each well. Then, to show the effect of doxorubicin on the cells, 10 μg·mL−1 (200 μL) of doxorubicin added to the cells and incubated for 1 and 2 h and then the images were recorded. Also, to count the number of unaffected cell using this method, the MKN 45 cell lines were cultured (Section 4.3) and treated with various concentrations of doxorubicin. Then the cell lines were incubated for 4 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2. After 2 h incubation, the doxorubicin treated cells were separated and suspended to the culture medium to count with the RRS based method. The DOX concentrations were 1, 2.5, 5.0, 7.5 and 10 μg·mL−1.

To investigate the cell viability, the 1.0 × 104 cells/well were firstly seeded on 96-well plates. After 24 h of incubation, the cells were treated with various concentrations of FA-N-GQDs and incubated for 24, 48 and 72 h. Then, the treated MKN 45 cells were washed with PBS buffer, and added 20 μL of MTT (5 mg·mL−1) solution to each well. The cells were incubated for 4 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2, and subsequently the media was removed and 200 μL of DMSO was added to each well and incubated for another 30 min. Finally, the MTT reader was applied to measure the absorbance at 570 nm with a reference wavelength of 630 nm.

DAPI staining test was carried out to show the effect of the nanoparticles to the nuclei of the MKN 45 cells where FA-N-GQDs possible induced apoptosis cause to fragmentation of the cell nuclei. In brief, MKN 45 cells were seeded in 6-well plates with a cell density of 5 × 105 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to grow the cancer cells. Then, the wells were treated with various concentrations of FA-N-GQDs which were filtered with a 0.22 μm sterile hydrophilic filter and incubated for another 48 h. Finally, cells were washed with PBS (three times) to eliminate any free FA-N-GQDs. In the next step, the 4% (w/w) paraformaldehyde was used to fix the cells at room temperature for 15 min. The cells were washed with PBS (three times) and Triton X-100 (0.1% w/v) was added to the permeabilization of the cell membranes where the incubation time was 5 min. Afterwards, the DAPI solution with a concentration of 300 mg·L−1 was added to each well and incubated for about 15 min. Finally, the DNA fragmentation of cancerous cells was observed using a fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups of FA, N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs were studied using FTIR spectroscopy. FTIR peaks approved effective attachments of the FA molecules to N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , the functional groups of the FA and N-GQDs are indicated. As demonstrated in Fig. 1, the characteristic peaks of the N-GQDs are appeared at 3245, 1638, 1076 and 1577 cm−1 of OH, NH2, CC and CO stretching-vibration peaks, respectively [38,39] which approve the availability of the OH, NH2 and COOH functional groups in the surface of the N-GQDs. Also, 1691 cm−1 as characteristic peak of FA is appeared. Investigation of the FTIR peak of the FA-N-GQDs is strongly confirmed the synthesis of FA-N-GQDs. As can be seen, the general shape of the FA-N-GQDs is mixed form of FA and N-GQDs. p‑Amino benzoic acid and aromatic ring stretch of the pyridine moieties as characteristic peaks of the FA were obviously seen in the range of 1470–1695 cm−1 along with the CH stretching peak at 2943 cm−1. The broad peak around 3500 and over 1630–1690 cm−1 are indicating of the amide NH and CO stretch absorption, respectively, which are imply the amide covalent linkage of FA with N-GQDs.

Zeta potential analysis of N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs was investigated to determine the surface charge and the stability of the synthesized material. As results revealed, the negative zeta potential value was changed to more negative potentials approving the attachment of FA groups where the negative charge have a major effect in stability of FA-N-GQDs [18,40]. Particle size of materials is normally observed by DLS. DLS analyses of N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs show an increase in the particle size of the FA-N-GQDs (Fig. 2 ).

The surface morphology of N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs were analyzed by FE-SEM (Fig. 3(a) and (b)). The FE-SEM images showed the sheet-like layer of graphene structure of FA-N-GQDs. The investigation of the elemental analysis of the prepared QDs was determined by energy dispersion spectroscopy (EDS) which are listed in Table 2 . Results affirmed that the main elements of N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs are C, N and O. Due to the anchoring of FA on the surface of N-GQDs, the percentages of N was decreased while the O percent was increased which approve the successful surface modification with FA and attaching of the FA to the NH2 groups of the surface N-GQDs. Further, the AFM images of the surface topography of N-GQDs were collected in Fig. 4 where the N-GQDs dots could be realized [41,42].

The TEM studies were carried out to determine the actual size of the N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs (Fig. 5 ). The TEM images obviously showed that the average particle sizes of the N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs are estimated to be 10 and 15 nm, respectively.

The XRD analysis was performed to confirm the structure of the FA-N-GQDs and N-GQDs from 3.0° to 80.0° (2θ) (Fig. 6 ). The XRD patterns of N-GQDs show wide peak located at around 14 and 24°. The broad peak around 24.5 is attributed to a (002) signal centered with 0.368 nm interlayer spacings. However, the d value is longer than the natural graphite which is ascribed to the adding of functional active groups on the surface of the GQDs and disordered stacking effect. Also, by functionalization of the surface of the N-GQDs with FA molecules an increase in the valve of d-spacing is observed which is related to the oxygen intercalation of the attached functional active groups whereas this can approve the correct attaching of FA molecules on the surface of N-GQDs [43,44].

Spectrophotometric study was investigated to confirm the effective anchoring of FA on N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 7A, there is an absorption peak of 265 nm related to the bonding of FA to NHS (green curve), which approves the first step of the synthesis of the FA-N-GQDs. Fig. 7B was confirmed the attachment of the NHS conjugated-folate on the surface of N-GQDs where the attaching of the FA cause to appearance an obvious spectrum at 350 nm. The absorption spectra at 230 nm and 280 nm can be assigned to the n → π* (CC) and π → π* (CO) transitions [45]. Also, the fluorimetric properties of the N-GQDs and FA-N-GQDs were investigated and presented in Fig. 8 . The fluorescence peak is 440 nm for both types of GQDs which is close to the reported value of 433 nm. The sp2 type structures and oxygen-rich functional groups (CO, CO and COOH) on the surface of N-GQDs are caused to the fluorescence emission [46].

The MKN 45 cells were treated with various concentrations of FA-N-GQDs nanoparticles to estimate the uptake ability of FA-N-GQDs. Then, the cells were incubated for 20 h and washed with buffer solution to remove unused FA-N-GQDs. After detaching of cells from the surface of the flasks the number of uptake cells was counted by flow cytometry. As results revealed, about 70% of the cells was affected by FA-N-GQDs. Concurrently, the same procedure was applied to show the qualitative uptake of FA-N-GQDs. The cellular uptake of FA-N-GQDs was further affirmed by fluorescence microscopy images. The fluorescence images of FA-N-GQDs treated MKN 45 cells are demonstrated in Fig. 9 . MKN 45 Cells were seeded in a 6-well tissue culture plate and then incubated for 24 h and treated with various concentrations of FA-N-GQDs and incubated for 20 h.

The toxicity of the synthesized nanomaterials is crucial for the possible application of the developed sensor, therefore the viability of the MKN 45 cells should be tested. In the present study, the toxicity of the FA-N-GQDs was tested by in vitro investigations. The MTT assay as standard method was used to estimate the cytotoxicity of FA-N-GQDs. The result was demonstrated in Fig. 10 . The cytotoxicity effect of the FA-N-GQDs on the MKN 45 cells was studied for 24, 48 and 72 h. MTT assay results obviously exhibited no apparent toxicity towards the MKN 45 cells and demonstrating the biocompatible nature of FA-N-GQDs. To approve the MTT assay results, DAPI staining test was utilized to show the condensing and fragmentation of the DNA of the dead cells. DAPI as fluorescent staining of nucleic acids can penetrate membranes of dead cells and visualize the apoptotic cells. As shown in Fig. 11 , in comparison with the control group (Fig. 11a), the addition of the FA-N-GQDs did not show any obvious toxic effects on the MKN 45 cells and fragmentations of DNA were not detected.

RRS spectra were recorded by utilizing of synchronous fluorescence spectroscopy where the wavelength interval (Δλ) was set to 0 nm [36,44]. The RRS spectra of FA decorated N-GQDs recorded for the different concentrations of MKN 45 cell are presented in Fig. 12A. The FA decorated N-GQDs exhibited two major RRS peaks at ~410 nm and ~535 nm. Actually, FA-N-GQDs should respond to the binding to the target FR-positive MKN 45 cells by the change RRS intensity. Preferably, the response of a sensor should be measurable to acquire the concentration related response of FR-positive cells where this demand is provided using FA-N-GQDs nanoparticles.

Since optimum condition of RRS is important for higher signal and consequently sensitivity of cytosensor, various effects were tested to reach the optimum RRS conditions. To pursue the optimal pH for attaching of FA-N-GQDs to FR-positive cells, various pH values were regarded as reaction medium pH and results presented in Fig. 13A. Due to the intrinsic properties of the live cell lines and to decrease the possible apoptosis of the cell lines, the pH 7.4 is regarded as buffering condition of the capturing medium.

Concentration of FA-N-GQDs is another parameter to affect the RRS intensities where results collected in Fig. 12B. As demonstrated, the maximum difference between FA-N-GQDs in the absence and presence of 10,000 cells/mL is observed at the 100 μL of FA-N-GQDs. The effect of incubation time to the reaction was also investigated in PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) (Fig. 12C). The RRS response increased to a maximum within 20 min and remained unchanged for about 5 min, however, after 30 min the RRS response was decreased. All cells were kept at 37 °C, culture medium and under dark medium to decrease the mortality rate of MKN 45 cells.

High affinity of FA to the folate bioreceptor cause to the formation of more strong bonds between FA assembled N-GQDs and the FRs of the cell membrane leads to the enhancement of the RRS of the FA decorated N-GQDs. Fig. 12A demonstrates the RRS spectra of the FA-N-GQDs probe by increasing the MKN 45 cell lines. As shown in Fig. 12B, a linear correlation between the enhanced RRS signal of the FA-N-GQDs and the number of MKN 45 was obtained in a wide range from 1.0 × 102 to 7.0 × 104 cells·mL−1. However the calibration curve for MCF 7 (Fig. 12(B-I)) and HT 29 (Fig. 12(B-II)) were 1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 104 and 5.0 × 102 to 4.0 × 104 cells·mL−1, respectively. Analytical figures of merit and repeatability of the developed cytosensor were collected in Tables 3 and 4
.

From Fig. 12, it can be concluded that the different manner of the FA-N-GQDs towards the various cell lines which arise from the characteristic properties of cells. Cells with higher density of FRs on their surfaces could be resulted in better sensitivities however the shape of cells and their spacing on the FA-N-GQDs are the other factors that may affect the capturing of cells with the QDs.

The specificity of the FA-N-GQDs nanocomposite towards the detection of MKN 45 (as a model cell) was evaluated using HEK 293 as normal cell. As demonstrated in Fig. 14 , in the presence of the HEK 293 in contact with the FA-N-GQDs nanomaterial, the minimum attachments of the nanomaterials is observed. Fig. 14(a–d) are showing microscopic images without and Fig. 14(e–h) with using fluorescence green wavelength. Compared to the images of the control group (Fig. 14e), the nanomaterial affected groups (Fig. 14f–h) didn't emit florescence under green fluorescence wavelength. These results obviously approve the selective capturing of the cancer cells which possess FR on their membranes and also the targeting capability of the FA-N-GQDs is confirmed by live cell line imaging. However, the fluorescence images of the MKN 45 cancer (Fig. 9), could obviously confirm the effective attaching of cells to the QDs by FRs-FA interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytosensing of MKN 45 after treatment with doxorubicin
ce:section.ce:para: The cytosensing ability of the developed method was evaluated using determination of treated MKN 45 live cell lines. The MKN 45 cell lines were exposed with various concentrations of doxorubicin and incubated for 2 h. Then, the cells were separated from wells and collected to the tubes for RRS-based determination. Fig. 15 shows the number of the non-affected cells with doxorubicin using the developed method. Due to the differences in the concentration of doxorubicin, number of the FR-positive cells will change however in samples with higher concentration of doxorubicin, and the RRS intensity is decreased which reflects the effective impact of doxorubicin. As fluorescence microscopy images are showing in Fig. 16 , at the higher concentrations of doxorubicin, the uptakes are high.

In this work, novel a resonance Rayleigh scattering based cytosensor for detection of the cancerous cells of gastric, intestinal and breast via selective bonding of the synthesized cell with the FR of the cellular membrane was developed. The surface of the N-GQDs functionalized by FA moieties to aid the specific bonding to the target cells. The abundance of the NH2 or NH groups on the layered-structure of the GQDs provides more active sites to bond between cell and the functionalize material where cause to achieve more selective and subsequently sensitive detection of FR-positive cancer cells. Moreover, the biocompatibility of different functionalized QDs based nanomaterials was tested by MTT assay. Evaluation of cellular uptakes showed the enhanced cellular internalization of FA-N-GQD in FA positive receptors of MKN 45, HT 29 and MCF 7 cells, suggesting selective attachment and uptake of FA functionalized N-GQDs and decreased uptake in off-target cells. The FA functionalized N-GQDs presented wide dynamic range, LLOQ and fast response time for the three reported tumor cells of MKN 45, HT 29 and MCF 7. The dynamic range and LLOQ of the constructed nanoplatform for tumor cells was equal to or better than other reported approaches. As an outstanding feature of the reported cytosensor is that the FA functionalized N-GQDs proposes an antibody-antigen interaction free based method which is free from the complexity of the assays. Further, doxorubicin molecules were also conjugated to the surface of FA bi-functionalized N-GQDs. In brief, a simple and effective cytosensor was proposed based on the very low toxic carbon based materials. Also, this scattering based cytosensor provides a sensitive and selective method to detect the cancerous cells where show simple operating system and have potential for designing early detection tools for cancer diagnostics in clinical investigations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial supports from Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran. This study was supported by Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Science as PhD thesis of J. Soleymani under Ethical Committee License of the National Institute for Medical Research Development (IR.NIMAD.REC.1396210).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Y. Sasaki, Methods for treatment of gastric cancer, U.S. Patent No. 9,512,223, 2016.
